shortDescription: The Dragon-Slaying Lance of Saint George

worshipPractices.name: Relic Veneration
worshipPractices.description: Ascalon, as a relic associated with Saint George, is venerated through physical touch, prayer, and display. Churches possessing fragments or supposed replicas of Ascalon are often visited by pilgrims seeking blessings and protection, especially from evil and dragons (both literal and metaphorical).

worshipPractices.name: Iconography and Art
worshipPractices.description: Images and artistic depictions of Saint George wielding Ascalon are common in churches and religious art. These images serve as visual reminders of the saint's victory over evil and the power of faith. The lance is often depicted prominently, emphasizing its role in the dragon's defeat.

festivals.name: Saint George's Day (April 23rd)
festivals.description: While not specifically focused solely on Ascalon, Saint George's Day celebrations often include reenactments of the dragon-slaying legend. These reenactments may feature a prop representing Ascalon, highlighting its importance in the narrative of Saint George's triumph.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ascalon, through its association with Saint George, continues to be a symbol of courage, faith, and the triumph of good over evil in modern culture. The image of the lance appears in heraldry, literature, and popular culture, often representing righteousness and divine intervention. Its legacy serves as an inspiration for individuals facing adversity.

name: Ascalon

sources.name: Jacobus de Voragine, *The Golden Legend*
sources.description: A 13th-century collection of hagiographies that includes a popular version of the Saint George legend.
sources.title: Jacobus de Voragine, *The Golden Legend*

sources.name: E.A. Wallis Budge, *The Life of Saint George*
sources.description: A compilation of various Coptic and Ethiopic versions of the Saint George story.
sources.title: E.A. Wallis Budge, *The Life of Saint George*

sources.name: Caxton's Version of the Golden Legend
sources.description: William Caxton's English translation of *The Golden Legend*, which helped popularize the Saint George narrative in England.
sources.title: Caxton's Version of the Golden Legend

sources.name: Ronald Hutton, *The Pagan Religions of the Ancient British Isles: Their Nature and Legacy*
sources.description: Provides context for the pre-Christian beliefs that Saint George and his lance Ascalon came to supplant.
sources.title: Ronald Hutton, *The Pagan Religions of the Ancient British Isles: Their Nature and Legacy*

sources.name: David Farmer, *The Oxford Dictionary of Saints*
sources.description: Offers a concise overview of Saint George's life and legend, including the significance of his lance.
sources.title: David Farmer, *The Oxford Dictionary of Saints*

extendedContent.content:
Ascalon is the name traditionally given to the lance or spear wielded by Saint
                    George in his legendary battle against the dragon. While the weapon is not named
                    in the earliest versions of the Saint George legend, later medieval traditions
                    gave it this name, possibly derived from the city of Ashkelon in the Levant,
                    where George was said to have originated.
                
                
                    The lance represents the archetypal weapon of the Christian knight - the primary
                    arm of a mounted warrior, used to strike the first and often decisive blow in
                    combat. In Saint George's hands, Ascalon became a symbol of faith's power to
                    overcome evil, the physical manifestation of spiritual warfare.
                
                
                    Unlike many legendary weapons that possess magical properties, Ascalon's power
                    in Christian tradition comes not from enchantment but from the righteousness
                    of its wielder and the blessing of God. It is a mortal weapon made sacred
                    through holy purpose.
extendedContent.title: Description and Nature

extendedContent.content:
The Dragon and the Princess
                
                    According to the most popular version of the legend (found in Jacobus de
                    Voragine's Golden Legend, c. 1260), a fearsome dragon lived near the city
                    of Silene in Libya (or sometimes Beirut or Cappadocia, depending on the
                    version). The dragon poisoned the countryside and demanded daily sacrifices
                    of sheep to appease its hunger.
                
                
                    When sheep grew scarce, the city began sacrificing humans, selected by lot.
                    One day the lot fell upon the king's daughter. Despite the king's pleas,
                    the princess was led to the dragon's lake to await her fate.
                

                Saint George's Intervention
                
                    George, a Roman soldier from Cappadocia and a Christian knight, happened upon
                    the princess awaiting the dragon. Learning of her plight, he resolved to
                    confront the monster. When the dragon emerged, George charged on horseback,
                    making the sign of the cross and commending himself to God.
                
                
                    With Ascalon, George struck the dragon a grievous blow, wounding it severely.
                    He then had the princess tie her girdle around the dragon's neck, and the
                    beast followed them meekly into the city. There, George promised to slay
                    the dragon if the people would convert to Christianity. When they agreed,
                    he killed the beast with his sword, and the king built a church on the spot.
extendedContent.title: The Legend of Saint George

extendedContent.content:
Triumph of Faith Over Evil
                
                    Ascalon represents the power of Christian faith to overcome evil. The dragon
                    in medieval Christian symbolism often represented Satan, paganism, or sin
                    itself. George's victory with Ascalon thus symbolizes the triumph of the
                    Church over its spiritual enemies.
                

                Chivalric Ideal
                
                    The lance of Saint George became the archetypal weapon of the ideal Christian
                    knight - the miles Christi or "soldier of Christ." Ascalon embodies the
                    chivalric virtues of courage, faith, and protection of the innocent that
                    medieval culture celebrated.
                

                National Symbolism
                
                    As Saint George became the patron saint of England, Portugal, Georgia, and
                    numerous other nations and cities, his lance became associated with national
                    identity. The image of George with Ascalon appears on coats of arms, flags,
                    and national symbolism throughout Europe.
                

                The Knight's Weapon
                
                    The choice of a lance rather than a sword for George's dragon-slaying reflects
                    the medieval understanding of martial hierarchy. The lance was the knight's
                    primary weapon, the one used for the initial charge that decided battles.
                    George's use of Ascalon marks him as a true knight.
extendedContent.title: Symbolism and Significance
Modern Depictions
extendedContent.content:
Guild Wars 2: Features Ascalon as a significant location
                    and Ascalonian weapons as a weapon set
                    Various RPGs: Saint George's lance appears as a holy
                    weapon effective against dragons and evil
                    Heraldry: The image of George with his lance remains
                    common in English, Georgian, and other national symbolism
                    Religious Art: Countless paintings, sculptures, and
                    icons depict George with Ascalon piercing the dragon

longDescription:

                    George in his legendary battle against the dragon. While the weapon is not named
                    in the earliest versions of the Saint George legend, later medieval traditions
                    gave it this name, possibly derived from the city of Ashkelon in the Levant,
                    where George was said to have originated.

description:
The Dragon-Slaying Lance of Saint George Ascalon is the name traditionally given to the lance or spear wielded by Saint
                    George in his legendary battle against the dragon. While the

keyMyths.title: The Dragon Fight at Silene
keyMyths.description: Saint George, armed with Ascalon, confronts a dragon terrorizing the Libyan city of Silene. After weakening the beast with his lance, he secured its surrender and led it into the town, promising to slay it only if the populace converted to Christianity.

keyMyths.title: Baptism of Silene
keyMyths.description: Following the dragon's subjugation, Ascalon is instrumental in the mass conversion of the people of Silene. George uses the lance as a symbol of his power and God's will, convincing the king and his subjects to abandon paganism.
keyMyths.source: Various versions of the Saint George legend, including Eastern Orthodox hagiographies

keyMyths.title: Symbol of Chivalry
keyMyths.description: Ascalon becomes associated with chivalric ideals. Knights throughout the Middle Ages viewed the lance as a representation of righteous combat, faith, and the protection of the innocent, emulating Saint George's bravery.

keyMyths.title: Relic Veneration
keyMyths.description: Although the physical existence of Ascalon is unconfirmed, some churches and monasteries claimed to possess fragments or replicas of the lance. These relics were venerated for their supposed protective powers and association with the saint.
keyMyths.source: Church records and accounts of relic veneration in the Middle Ages

keyMyths.title: Ascalon's Divine Aid
keyMyths.description: The lance Ascalon is often described as being blessed or imbued with divine power, allowing Saint George to overcome the dragon, which is portrayed as a symbol of evil or paganism. This divine assistance highlights the saint's role as a champion of Christianity.
keyMyths.source: Hagiographical accounts of Saint George's life and miracles

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Gungnir, Odin's spear, is a powerful weapon associated with a major deity, similar to Ascalon being associated with Saint George and dragon slaying.

cross_cultural_parallels.entity: The Bow and Arrows of Apollo
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Apollo's bow and arrows are divine weapons used to defeat monstrous beings and inflict harm, paralleling Ascalon's role in slaying a dragon.
Vijaya is Shiva's bow used in the destruction of Tripura. Like Ascalon, it is a weapon of divine origin used against formidable foes.
Totsuka-no-Tsurugi is the sword used by Susano-o to slay the Yamata no Orochi, a monstrous serpent, much like Ascalon's use against a dragon.

associations.name: Steel
associations.significance: Represents the strength and purity of Saint George's faith, forged in the battle against evil.

associations.name: Divine Intervention
associations.significance: Ascalon symbolizes God's aid in overcoming seemingly insurmountable evil.

associations.name: Red
associations.significance: Represents the blood shed in the fight against evil and the martyrdom of Saint George.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: Represents the singularity of purpose in fighting against evil, and the single spear used to slay the dragon.

associations.name: Star
associations.significance: Ascalon, like a star, guides and inspires those who fight for good.

associations.name: Courage
associations.significance: Ascalon is a symbol of the courage needed to confront and defeat evil, even when facing overwhelming odds.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: Represents the dragon, the embodiment of evil and the antithesis of Saint George's virtue, slain by the lance.

companions.name: Saint George
companions.description: Saint George is the wielder of Ascalon, relying on its divine power to defeat the dragon. The lance is an extension of his holy mission.

companions.name: The White Horse
companions.description: The white horse carries Saint George into battle, enabling him to reach the dragon and wield Ascalon effectively. It symbolizes purity and divine support.

companions.name: The Princess
companions.description: The Princess is the damsel saved from the dragon and supports Saint George's quest. She is the reason for his heroism.

companions.name: God
companions.description: The lance Ascalon is bestowed with divine power from God. He guides and blesses Saint George's use of Ascalon.

subtitle: Saint George's legendary lance, forged for dragon slaying.

wielders.name: Jesus Christ
wielders.context: Source of the lance's divine power
